did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The study used a trained convolutional neural network (CNN) to provide AI support to dermatologists for the task of dichotomous image-based discrimination between melanoma and nevus in dermoscopic images. The CNN was trained on images from the ISIC archive and HAM10000 dataset, and its predictions were used to evaluate the effect of AI assistance on dermatologists' diagnostic accuracy.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper is explicitly labeled as an 'Original Paper' and a 'Web-Based Survey Study'. It describes original experimental methods involving 12 board-certified dermatologists classifying dermoscopic images with and without AI support, reports specific statistical results (sensitivity, specificity, accuracy, p-values), and draws new conclusions from this primary data collection, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Artificial Intelligence and Its Effect on Dermatologists’ Accuracy in Dermoscopic Melanoma Image Classification: Web-Based Survey Study
brief_summary: This web-based survey study investigated whether artificial intelligence support improves the diagnostic accuracy of dermatologists in the dichotomous classification of dermoscopic images as melanoma or nevus. Twelve board-certified dermatologists classified disjoint sets of 100 unique dermoscopic images twice: first based on personal experience alone and second with the assistance of a trained convolutional neural network (CNN). The CNN was trained on images from the ISIC archive and the HAM10000 dataset. The main evaluation approach compared the dermatologists' sensitivity, specificity, and accuracy between the two conditions using statistical tests. The results showed that while specificity remained unchanged, mean sensitivity increased significantly from 59.4% to 74.6% and mean accuracy increased from 65.0% to 73.6% with AI support. The study concluded that AI support can significantly improve the overall accuracy of dermatologists in melanoma image classification.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the two classes in the dichotomous classification task. Images were selected from the ISIC archive and HAM10000 dataset. In the training set construction, there were 1608 melanoma images (after duplication to balance classes) and 1633 total available in the archive subset.

One of the two classes in the dichotomous classification task. Images were selected from the ISIC archive and HAM10000 dataset. In the training set construction, there were 3286 nevus images. Dermatologists classified lesions as 'benign' or 'malignant', where nevus corresponds to the benign class.

all_task_types.target: Dichotomous diagnosis of dermoscopic images as melanoma or nevus (benign vs malignant)
all_task_types.details: The study involves a trained convolutional neural network (CNN) performing binary classification to discriminate between biopsy-verified melanoma and nevus. The model outputs a binary decision (melanoma or nevus) and a confidence level mapped from raw output probability to a 0-100% range. Dermatologists also performed binary classification tasks.

datasets_used.name: ISIC Archive
datasets_used.description: A publicly accessible archive containing anonymous dermoscopic images from multiple sources and various camera systems. Images were selected from this archive for both training the CNN and constructing the test sets.

use.training: Source of images for constructing the 24 training sets (after removing test set images) and the validation set for the CNN.
use.validation: Source of images for the evenly balanced validation set used for calibration and validation of the CNN.
use.testing: Source of the 1200 unique dermoscopic images (50 melanomas and 50 nevi per set) used in the web-based survey for dermatologists to classify.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000
datasets_used.description: A large dataset of dermoscopic images included within the ISIC archive. A large fraction of the images used in this study came from this dataset.

use.training: Source of images for constructing the 24 training sets for the CNN.
use.validation: Source of images for the validation set for the CNN.
use.testing: Source of the 1200 unique dermoscopic images used in the web-based survey.

datasets_used.name: Study Test Sets
datasets_used.description: 1200 unique dermoscopic images split into 12 non-overlapping individual test sets containing 100 images each (50 melanomas and 50 nevi). Each set was assigned to one dermatologist.

use.testing: Used as the primary data source for the dermatologists to classify in the web-based survey (Part I without AI, Part II with AI).

datasets_used.name: CNN Training Sets
datasets_used.description: 24 training sets constructed for each of the 12 test sets by removing the test set images from the total image data (ISIC/HAM10000). Each training set contained 4894 images. Class imbalance was addressed by duplicating melanoma images.

use.training: Used to train the convolutional neural network (CNN) classifiers.

datasets_used.name: CNN Validation Sets
datasets_used.description: 24 evenly balanced validation sets, one for each training set, removed from the training data for calibration and validation purposes.

use.validation: Used for calibration and validation of the CNN classifiers.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To test for significance in changes in sensitivity and specificity with AI support.
To investigate whether dermatologists experienced a learning effect when working with AI support.
To correct the significance threshold for multiple testing (primary and secondary endpoints).
To estimate uncertainty for mean sensitivity, specificity, accuracy, confidence levels, and proportions of switching behavior.

hypothesis_testing.test: Wilcoxon test (for sensitivity and specificity); Pearson’s chi-squared test (for learning effect)
hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no difference in dermatologists' sensitivity, specificity, or accuracy when using AI support compared to personal experience alone; there is no learning effect when working with AI support.
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a difference in dermatologists' sensitivity, specificity, or accuracy when using AI support compared to personal experience alone; there is a learning effect when working with AI support.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: Statistical significance was considered at P<.05, corrected to 0.016 using Bonferroni correction for the primary and secondary endpoints. The study found that mean sensitivity increased significantly (P=.003) and mean accuracy increased significantly (P=.002) with AI support, while specificity did not change significantly (P=.54).

Study Test Sets (1200 unique images, 12.notes: Mean sensitivity and accuracy increased significantly with AI support (P=.003 and P=.002, respectively). Specificity did not change significantly (P=.54). The CNN's performance was comparable to its validation set performance (mean sensitivity 78.0%, specificity 81.0%, accuracy 80.0%).

Switching Behavior Analysis (n=94 cases.notes: Dermatologists showed a higher willingness to switch from benign to malignant (72%; 95% CI 53.4%-90.9%) than from malignant to benign (28%; 95% CI 9.1%-46.6%). In cases of disagreement, dermatologists altered their decision in 43% (15/35; 95% CI 30.8%-56.3%) of cases.

Confidence Distribution Analysis.notes: In cases of agreement, dermatologists' confidence increased substantially from Part I to Part II (67.0% to 79.1%). In cases of disagreement, confidence decreased (61.7% to 44.3%). The mean confidence of the classifier was higher when correct (71.7%) than when incorrect (34.8%).

architecture_details.details: The paper describes the model only as a 'trained convolutional neural network (CNN)' and references 'Multimedia Appendix 1' for a detailed description of the classifier training. Specific architectural details such as backbone, layers, heads, patch size, or parameter counts are not reported in the provided text.

training_details.training_set_size: 4894 images per training set (24 total training sets constructed)
training_details.balancing_method: Melanoma images were duplicated to counter class imbalance
training_details.augmentation: Online data augmentation was used to increase diversity of training data and modify duplicated images
training_details.validation: Evenly balanced validation set removed from each training set for calibration and validation
training_details.performance_on_validation: Mean sensitivity 78.0%, mean specificity 81.0%, mean accuracy 80.0%

classification_or_output_head.confidence_measure: Raw output probability for the predicted class mapped to a percentage range from 0% to 100%

preprocessing_techniques: Online data augmentation used to increase diversity of training data and modify duplicated images

Quality control step involving personal rating of image quality (excellent, good, sufficient, poor, other image problems, no image visible) which resulted in varying numbers of images rated by each dermatologist

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Used to increase the diversity of the training data and to modify duplicated images (melanoma images were duplicated to counter class imbalance). Specific geometric or color transform parameters were not reported in the provided text; details were referenced in Multimedia Appendix 1.

conclusions: AI support significantly improves the overall diagnostic accuracy of dermatologists in the dichotomous image-based discrimination between melanoma and nevus.
Mean sensitivity increased significantly from 59.4% to 74.6% (P=.003) with AI support.
Mean accuracy increased significantly from 65.0% to 73.6% (P=.002) with AI support.
Mean specificity remained almost unchanged (70.6% vs 72.4%; P=.54) with AI support.
Dermatologists altered their decisions in 43% of cases where they disagreed with the AI classifier.
Dermatologists showed a higher willingness to switch from a benign to a malignant diagnosis (72%) than from malignant to benign (28%) when AI disagreed with their initial assessment.
Dermatologists' confidence in their decisions increased when the CNN confirmed their decision and decreased when the CNN disagreed, even when the dermatologists were correct.
The findings support the argument for AI-based tools to aid clinicians in skin lesion classification.
The study provides a rationale for future research into AI classifiers in real-life settings where clinicians can integrate additional patient information such as age and medical history.
important_remarks: The study population consisted exclusively of 12 board-certified dermatologists, excluding trainees or non-experts, which limits the generalizability of findings to the broader clinical workforce.

The test images were biopsy-verified, representing 'edge cases' that are naturally more difficult to diagnose, which likely contributed to the lower baseline sensitivity (59.4%) compared to typical clinical settings.

The task was dichotomous classification (melanoma vs. nevus) rather than a therapy decision (biopsy vs. reassure); in real life, dermatologists may exhibit higher sensitivity due to a tendency to excise lesions when uncertain.

The CNN was trained on data from the ISIC archive and HAM10000 dataset, raising potential concerns about dataset bias and external validity if the test set distribution differs from the training distribution.

Specific architectural details of the CNN were not reported in the main text, with references made to Multimedia Appendix 1, limiting reproducibility and transparency regarding the model's complexity.

The study design was a web-based survey, which does not reflect real-life clinical workflows where clinicians can integrate additional patient information (e.g., age, medical history) alongside dermoscopic images.

There is a risk that AI support could be harmful if the classifier is incorrect, as it may decrease dermatologist confidence and lead them to switch from a correct to an incorrect diagnosis (observed in 39% of cases where dermatologists were correct and AI was incorrect).
The small sample size of participants (n=12) may limit the statistical power to detect subtle effects or interactions, although the study used Bonferroni correction for multiple testing.

The CNN's performance on the test set (sensitivity 84.7%, specificity 79.1%) was comparable to its validation set performance, suggesting no significant overfitting, but the specific validation methodology details are external to the main text.

Dermatologists showed a bias towards switching from benign to malignant diagnoses (72%) more often than malignant to benign (28%) when AI disagreed, indicating a potential over-reliance on AI for detecting malignancy.
